title: InductionBench: LLMs Fail in the Simplest Complexity Class
authors: Wenyue Hua, Tyler Wong, Sun Fei, Liangming Pan, Adam Jardine, William Yang Wang
venue: ACL 2025 main conference (long paper), ACL Anthology ID 2025.acl-long.1287 (per header notes: confirmed via ACL Anthology; this venue/acceptance info does not appear in the arXiv abs page metadata itself — a metadata gap, not a contradiction). arXiv 2502.15823 (v1 2025-02-20, current v4 2025-05-13), cs.LG.
claim: LLMs are evaluated on InductionBench, a benchmark that tests whether models can induce a string-to-string transformation function from a finite set of input-output pairs (inductive reasoning), grounded in the subregular hierarchy (ISL, L-OSL, R-OSL classes) which are provably learnable in polynomial time from polynomially-sized samples. The central finding is that even the most advanced models (including o3-mini) struggle to master these simplest complexity classes within the subregular hierarchy, and often fail to improve — sometimes degrade — as more (theoretically helpful) data is provided, revealing a fundamental deficiency in current LLMs' inductive reasoning capacity rather than a benchmark-difficulty artifact.
novelty: Authors position their contribution against prior inductive-reasoning evaluations (symbolic regression, artificial language translation, concept learning) which lack: (1) explicit analysis of task difficulty, (2) a guarantee that the provided I/O dataset can identify the target function, and (3) a mechanism to check whether the model's hypothesis is minimal (Occam's Razor / MDL). InductionBench addresses all three by grounding tasks in the subregular hierarchy (ISL/L-OSL/R-OSL, and an exploratory IOSL class with no known poly-time learning algorithm), using a formally defined 'characteristic sample' that provably suffices for learnability, and scoring not just correctness (compatibility) but also whether the model's rule set is the unique minimal (up to isomorphism) representation (precision/recall against the ground-truth rule set). They also emphasize: automated evaluation (no human annotation needed), dynamic/refreshable data generation (anti-memorization), and precise control over hypothesis-space size via k (context window), |Sigma| (vocab size), and number of minimal rules (Sec 2 'Our Contribution').

protocol.description: Single-shot / single-forward-pass per instance: the model receives one prompt containing the function class, k, alphabet, and a sample dataset of I/O pairs, and must produce a rule-based hypothesis in one response (zero-shot CoT). A separate 'Impact of Examples' sub-experiment (Sec 5.2) varies the *number of few-shot in-context demonstration examples* (1-shot/2-shot/3-shot) shown before the query, but this is still a single-call setup, not a multi-turn interactive loop — there is no feedback-and-revise dialogue with the model across turns.
protocol.observation_content: Per Sec 3.5/4/Table row headers, the prompt provides: (1) the function class (ISL / L-OSL / R-OSL / IOSL), (2) the context window k, (3) the alphabet Sigma, and (4) the sample dataset D of input-output pairs (the 'characteristic sample', optionally scaled to 1x/2x/3x/4x its minimal size). The model is asked to 'produce a minimal rule-based description that reproduces the provided sample set' (Sec 3.5/4). Output format is the paper's own rule notation: 'condition ∘ target character → output of the target character' (Sec 3.5), e.g. 'a∘a→λ, b∘a→b'. Evaluation is zero-shot chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting ('We evaluate using zero-shot chain-of-thought prompting on six SOTA LLMs' — Sec 5, Experiment Setting). No demo-then-query held-out transformation string is requested directly; rather the model must state the general rule set, which is then (a) compared rule-by-rule to ground truth for precision/recall and (b) applied to the sample D to compute compatibility.

representations: The paper's own rule-based notation (condition ∘ target → output) is used as the canonical output format 'to streamline the generation and parsing of function representations' and because it 'simplifies both the model's output generation ... and our subsequent evaluation' (Sec 3.5). No alternative prompt/representation formats (e.g., natural language description vs. code vs. FST) are compared against each other for accuracy impact in the extraction; the representation axes that ARE varied and shown to affect accuracy are: number of in-context I/O examples (few-shot count: 0/1/2/3-shot, Sec 5.2 'Impact of Examples', Tables 8-10), and sample-set size relative to the minimal characteristic sample (1x-5x multiples, Sec 5.2 'Robustness', Figures 5-6), plus a contrast between adding genuinely new datapoints vs. simply repeating/padding context length (Figs 5 vs 6) to isolate context-length effects from new-information effects.

models.name: o3-mini
models.key_results: Best model overall. Table 1 (k=4,|Sigma|=4,rules=3,sample=2x): recall 73.33/66.67/63.33, precision 59.58/69.17/62.00, compatibility 10.00/10.00/30.00 for ISL/L-OSL/R-OSL respectively — only model with nonzero compatibility in that hard setting. Table 3 (Standard Leaderboard, linear weight): recall 28.93, precision 43.12, compatibility 5.69 — 'even o3-mini achieves only a 5.69% compatibility score' (Sec 6.1) because none of the models succeed at k=4. Table 4 (log-weighted leaderboard): recall 57.58, precision 63.89, compatibility 33.93. Table 2 (harder k in {4,5}, |Sigma|=5): compatibility is 0.00 in nearly every k=4/k=5, rules=4/5 cell for o3-mini, with exactly one exception (R-OSL, rules=4, k=4: compatibility = 10.00); all six of o3-mini's k=5 cells (ISL/L-OSL/R-OSL x rules=4/5) are 0.00 — so compatibility does fully collapse to 0 at k=5 for o3-mini, but not because of an L-OSL k=4/k=5 swap (that nonzero L-OSL rules=4,k=5=10.00 cell belongs to o1-mini's row, not o3-mini's).

models.name: o1-mini
models.key_results: Second-best model. Table 1: recall 36.67/43.33/26.67, precision 22.09/32.12/17.58, compatibility 0.00/0.00/0.00 across ISL/L-OSL/R-OSL. Table 2: nonzero recall/precision but compatibility at or near 0 across nearly all harder k=4/5,|Sigma|=5 settings. 'Restricted Access to the o1 Model' is listed as a paper Limitation — o1 family investigation was hindered by limited availability/compute.

models.name: GPT-4o
strategies: few-shot (0/1/2/3-shot variant, Llama-3.3-70B only for the few-shot sweep — GPT-4o not part of that sub-experiment)
models.key_results: Table 1 (k=4,|Sigma|=4,rules=3,sample=2x): recall 10.00/13.33/16.67, precision 2.67/3.82/6.73, compatibility 0.00/0.00/0.00 for ISL/L-OSL/R-OSL. Appendix Table 5 (ISL, sample=2, easier settings e.g. k=2,vocab=2,rules=1): recall 60.00, precision 43.33, compatibility 40.00 — degrades sharply as k increases to 4 (compatibility often 0).

models.name: DeepSeek-V3
models.key_results: Table 1: recall 13.33/23.33/3.33, precision 2.46/2.73/0.25, compatibility 0.00/0.00/0.00 (ISL/L-OSL/R-OSL). Table 3 leaderboard not listed for DeepSeek-V3 directly (leaderboard uses DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B instead: recall 3.84, precision 5.14, compatibility 0.78).

models.name: Llama-3.3-70B (FP8)
strategies: few-shot sweep (1/2/3-shot demonstrations, Sec 5.2 'Impact of Examples', Tables 8-10)
models.key_results: Table 1: recall 10.00/10.00/10.00, precision 5.32/6.33/10.83, compatibility 0.00/0.00/0.00 (ISL/L-OSL/R-OSL). Table 3 leaderboard: recall 6.55, precision 5.76, compatibility 0.12. Few-shot sweep (Table 8, ISL, vocab=2, rules=1): compatibility rises from 60.00 (0-shot) to 60/70/80 (1/2/3-shot) at k=2, but at k=4 stays near 0-10 regardless of shot count — 'as the complexity increases ... the benefits of additional few-shot examples become negligible' (Sec 5.2).

models.name: Llama-3.1-8B
models.key_results: Table 3 leaderboard: recall 0.00, precision 0.00, compatibility 0.00 — complete floor.

models.name: Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct
models.key_results: Table 3 leaderboard: recall 7.26, precision 0.66, compatibility 0.03. Table 4 (log-weighted): recall 7.48, precision 6.60, compatibility 0.48.

models.name: DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B
models.key_results: Table 3 leaderboard: recall 3.84, precision 5.14, compatibility 0.78. Table 4 (log-weighted): recall 23.17, precision 24.66, compatibility 8.63.

headline_numbers: Table 1 (k=4,|Sigma|=4,rules=3,sample size=2x characteristic sample): compatibility collapses to 0.00 for all 6 models except o3-mini across all three classes ISL/L-OSL/R-OSL (o3-mini: 10.00/10.00/30.00).

Table 3 (Standard Leaderboard, 1,080 questions, linear weighting): best model o3-mini scores only 5.69% weighted average compatibility, 28.93% recall, 43.12% precision, driven down because 'none of the models succeed on tasks where k=4' and those settings receive disproportionately large weight (Sec 6.1).
Table 4 (log-weighted leaderboard, same 1,080 questions): o3-mini improves to 33.93% compatibility, 57.58% recall, 63.89% precision when k=4's dominance is dampened by log-weighting.
Sec 5.2 'Impact of k': moving k from 2 to 4 'markedly reduces recall, precision, and compatibility' across all models — the single largest difficulty driver identified.

Sec 5.2 'Robustness'/Figure 5: average compatibility 'decreases steeply as the number of provided input-output examples increases' beyond the minimal characteristic sample — genuinely new datapoints (not just longer context) drive this, since repeating the same characteristic sample (Figure 6) causes only a small decline even at 3x-5x multiples, whereas adding new distinct datapoints causes compatibility to 'plummet nearly to zero for multiples of 4 and 5' (Sec 5.2).

Sec 6.2 Exploration Leaderboard (IOSL class, no known poly-time learning algorithm): introduced specifically to rule out the possibility that any observed success reflects a known 'hack' algorithm rather than genuine induction; details of IOSL leaderboard model scores are not present in the extracted body text beyond definition/setup (see corpus_claim_verdict note).
failure_modes: Sec 5.2 'Error Type Analysis' (Figure 7, three models: Llama3.3-70B, o1-mini, o3-mini) categorizes errors into Missing Rules (Completely Missed [most common] > Too General > Too Specific [rare]) and Wrong Rules (Completely Wrong [most common] > Too General > Too Specific > Correct Condition but Wrong Transformation [rare]). Summary: 'missing rules are most frequently completely missed or too general, while wrong rules often end up completely wrong or again too general. Only a small fraction are too specific or feature a correct condition but wrong transformation, indicating that once models identify the right condition, they typically produce the correct transformation' (Sec 5.3). Robustness failure: performance degrades (does not improve or stabilize) as more genuinely new data is added, which the authors interpret as evidence the model 'appears to become confused or overwhelmed' rather than performing 'robustly inductive' reasoning (Sec 5.2 'Robustness'). Increasing the number of minimal rules 'can substantially affect compatibility' especially at large k/|Sigma|, sometimes causing scores to plummet to near 0 when a second or third rule is added (Sec 5.2 'Impact of the Number of Rules').
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's entire main experiment is single forward-pass (zero-shot CoT): the model gets one prompt with the sample data and must emit a full rule-set hypothesis in one shot; there is no multi-turn feedback/revision loop tested anywhere in the extracted text. Within that single-shot design, the paper does vary demonstration count (0/1/2/3-shot in-context examples, still delivered in one prompt) and finds few-shot examples help only in simple settings ('few-shot examples do not help much' past a complexity threshold — Sec 5.3) and, separately, that giving the model MORE raw sample data (not multi-turn feedback, just a bigger one-shot context) makes performance WORSE, not better, contradicting the theoretical expectation that more characteristic-sample data should facilitate recovery of the true function (Sec 5.2 Robustness, Sec 5.3). The paper draws no conclusions about interactive/multi-turn settings since none were tested; it only concludes that current LLMs' one-shot inductive reasoning is not 'robustly inductive' and is fragile to both increased context-window complexity (k) and increased genuinely-novel in-context data.

relation_to_candidate.position: InductionBench is strictly single-shot / single-forward-pass (zero-shot CoT prompt in, rule-set hypothesis out) with no interactive/multi-turn feedback loop. What the model observes is the function class name, k, alphabet Sigma, and a sample I/O dataset (the paper's chosen representation is its own rule notation, not raw few-shot demo-then-query as in typical ICL benchmarks — though a few-shot demonstration-count sweep exists within the single-shot format). Output shape is a full symbolic rule-set description (not a single transformed string, and not a multiple-choice label) — this is closer to program/grammar induction output than to c23's per-instance short-string output. The verifier is a three-part symbolic metric (precision, recall against the unique ground-truth minimal rule set; and compatibility = whether applying the predicted rules to the sample set reproduces all given outputs), not a single exact-match on a held-out query transformation.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both are grounded in the same formal object: subregular string-to-string transduction (ISL/L-OSL/R-OSL are explicitly named as one of c23's five rule families per c23.md). Both use randomly generated, seeded functions/rule sets over small alphabets to guarantee contamination-resistance and precise difficulty control (k, |Sigma|, number of rules in InductionBench directly maps to c23's 'K independent latent rules' and difficulty ladder). Both treat the number of rules/complexity as a difficulty dial with a documented degradation pattern (InductionBench: rules and k both increase difficulty, more so k). c23's implementation plan explicitly vendors InductionBench's own generator/oracle code (synthetic_data_generation.py) as its codebase source.
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Output format: InductionBench asks for a full explicit rule-set description graded against ground truth by set-overlap metrics (precision/recall/compatibility), not a per-query transformed short string or 4-way multiple choice graded by exact match, which is what c23 proposes. (2) InductionBench does not test held-out generalization to a query string with multiple-choice distractors wrong in exactly one rule -- it only tests reconstructing rules that already explain the given sample D (compatibility is with the training sample itself, not a genuinely held-out test item). (3) InductionBench's single-shot design has no demo-then-one-query structure per instance in the way c23 specifies; the whole sample IS the input, and the model must produce the general function, not apply it to one new item. (4) c23 additionally spans casing/length rules, positional swaps, per-primitive command-to-action mappings, and 1D-ARC-style array transforms -- InductionBench covers only the subregular string-substitution/deletion classes (ISL/L-OSL/R-OSL/IOSL). (5) InductionBench's own reported ceiling is very low (o3-mini: 5.69% weighted compatibility on its full leaderboard, Table 3) -- a direct empirical reachability warning for any task, like c23, built on this same difficulty family.
relation_to_candidate.c23_would_add: A held-out-query, exact-match-on-transformed-output protocol (or 4-way multiple choice with distractors each wrong in exactly one rule) rather than InductionBench's rule-set-reconstruction-and-verify-against-training-sample protocol -- this makes grading trivial (single string/label compare) versus InductionBench's need for symbolic rule-set diffing. c23 also broadens beyond pure subregular ISL/OSL edits to a multi-family ladder (casing, swaps, command-mappings, 1D-ARC), and explicitly curates rule complexity to keep a reachable ceiling, addressing the reachability risk this paper's own numbers surface for the InductionBench-style stratum.

corpus_claim: InductionBench (Hua et al., ACL 2025) is c23's central design driver: subregular ISL/OSL string-function induction from I/O demos, with the reachability caveat that frontier models fail even the simplest complexity class. It also doubles as c23's codebase source.
corpus_claim_verdict: supported. The paper is precisely subregular (ISL, L-OSL, R-OSL, plus exploratory IOSL) string-to-string function induction from a sample of input-output pairs (Sec 3-4), and its own framing repeatedly states that even its best model (o3-mini) fails to reliably solve these 'simplest complexity classes within the subregular hierarchy' -- e.g. the title itself is 'LLMs Fail in the Simplest Complexity Class', Table 1 shows compatibility collapsing to 0 for 5 of 6 models even at moderate difficulty (k=4,|Sigma|=4,rules=3), and the Standard Leaderboard (Table 3) shows o3-mini at only 5.69% weighted compatibility. The paper's abstract explicitly states code and data are available at the GitHub repo (github.com/Wenyueh/inductive_reasoning_benchmark), matching the 'codebase source' half of the claim (confirmed reachable per the paper-record header). One nuance: InductionBench's I/O 'demos' are not few-shot demo-then-query pairs in the classic sense -- the entire sample set IS what the model must explain via an explicit rule-set output, not apply to a single held-out query -- so 'I/O demos' should be read as 'sample dataset used for function induction,' not literally as a few-shot-prompt-plus-query format like c23 proposes.
baseline_relevant: Instance/difficulty dials used: context window k in {2,3,4} (main), extended to {4,5} for stronger models with |Sigma|=5; alphabet size |Sigma| in {2,3,4} (main), extended to {5,6,7,8} for the leaderboards; number of minimal rules in {2,3,4} (main experiments) or {3,4,5} (leaderboards); sample size as a multiple (1x-4x, up to 5x in robustness figures) of the minimal 'characteristic sample' (Sec 4-5).

Prompt format that swung accuracy most: the rule-based notation 'condition ∘ target → output' (Sec 3.5) was specifically adopted because verbose functional/case-based descriptions are harder to generate and parse; k (context window) was the single largest accuracy-degrading factor, more so than vocabulary size |Sigma| (Sec 5.2).

Few-shot in-context example count (0/1/2/3-shot) helps only in low-complexity settings (small k, small |Sigma|) and has 'negligible' / diminishing benefit as complexity grows (Sec 5.2, 5.3) -- directly relevant to whether c23's few-shot demo axis will actually move accuracy at harder strata.

Metrics: precision = |R∩P|/|P|, recall = |R∩P|/|R|, compatibility = binary (all sample I/O pairs reproduced by predicted rule set) (Sec 5, Evaluation Metrics); a naive 'memorize every pair as its own rule' trivially achieves perfect compatibility but near-zero precision, so precision guards against degenerate lookup-table solutions -- a caution for designing c23's own scoring if it ever grades partial rule credit rather than pure exact-match.

Floors/ceilings observed: compatibility floor is literally 0.00 for most non-frontier models even at moderate difficulty (Table 1); best-model ceiling on the full leaderboard is only 5.69% weighted compatibility (Table 3, linear weight) or 33.93% (Table 4, log weight) -- i.e., even curated, this whole task family has a documented very-low ceiling at the paper's chosen difficulty settings, which is the load-bearing risk c23.md itself flags ('Ceiling unreachable ... without deliberate complexity curation').

Robustness/contamination-relevant finding: giving the model MORE novel sample data (beyond the minimal characteristic sample) makes compatibility drop sharply (Sec 5.2 Robustness, Figs 5-6) -- a caution that 'more demos' is not free lunch and should be calibrated, not maximized, when designing c23's few-shot demo count.

Data-release/contamination status: dataset is synthetically, dynamically generated from randomly sampled functions per the three/four parameters, explicitly designed to 'allow periodic refreshes to prevent models from relying on memorized examples' (Sec 2, 'Our Contribution' -- Dynamic Data Generation) -- directly supports c23's own contamination-proofing rationale via invented vocabularies and randomized rule subsets.

Codebase reachability: repo github.com/Wenyueh/inductive_reasoning_benchmark confirmed publicly reachable (HTTP 200 / GitHub API resolved) as of the paper-record fetch date 2026-07-22, per the paper-record header notes -- but note this dossier does not re-verify the c23.md claims of import-time crashes or seed non-determinism in that repo; those are reported in c23.md's own repo-review section, not in this paper extraction.
quotes: "Our experimental findings reveal that even the most advanced models available struggle to master the simplest complexity classes within the subregular hierarchy of functions, highlighting a notable deficiency in current LLMs' inductive reasoning capabilities." (Abstract)

"We evaluate using zero-shot chain-of-thought prompting on six SOTA LLMs, including Llama-3.3-70b ... Llama-3.1-405b ... GPT-4o ... DeepSeek-V3 ... o1-mini ... and o3-mini." (Sec 5, Experiment Setting)

"To streamline the generation and parsing of function representations, we employ a simplified notation wherein each transformation is written as 'condition ∘ target character → output of the target character'" (Sec 3.5, Rule-based Representation)
"As seen in the table, compatibility scores collapse to 0 for all models except o3-mini, which also achieves relatively modest compatibility overall." (Sec 5.1, discussing Table 1)
"Notably, even o3-mini achieves only a 5.69% compatibility score, largely because none of the models succeed on tasks where k=4." (Sec 6.1, Standard Leaderboard)

"Figure 5 illustrates how average compatibility decreases steeply as the number of provided input-output examples increases. This drop suggests that the LLM's reasoning process is not robustly inductive..." (Sec 5.2, Robustness)

"Few-shot examples do not help much: Few-shot examples help in simpler configurations but yield diminishing returns as k adn |Sigma| grows..." (Sec 5.3, Summary of findings) [verbatim typo 'adn' preserved from source]
"Coda and data are available https://github.com/Wenyueh/inductive_reasoning_benchmark." (Abstract, verbatim typo 'Coda' for 'Code' preserved per paper-record header note)

corrections: models[0] (o3-mini) key_results, Table 2 sub-claim: fixed a column/row misattribution. The dossier previously claimed the lone non-zero-collapse exception in Table 2 was 'L-OSL rules=4,k=4' and attributed it to o3-mini, and asserted 'table shows 0.00 for k=5' for that cell. Source Table 2 actually shows: (a) the L-OSL rules=4 non-zero compatibility cell (10.00) belongs to o1-mini's row, not o3-mini's, and occurs at k=5 (not k=4, which is 0.00 -- exactly backwards from the original text); (b) o3-mini's own actual exception is a different cell entirely: R-OSL, rules=4, k=4 = 10.00 (all of o3-mini's other Table 2 cells, including all six k=5 cells, are 0.00). Corrected the key_results text to identify the right model, right class, right k value, and right cell for each of the two true non-zero exceptions in Table 2.
